title: David A. Marinelarena
text:
DAVID A. MARINELARENA
"BIOLOGICAL RESEARCHER · PhD STUDENT ASU SOLS"










// about

I’m a Computational Biologist and Biotechnology Professional, currently an MCB PhD student at Arizona State University.
My research sits at the intersection of computational biology, microbiome science, multi-omics, bioinformatics, and machine learning — developing and applying computational methods to analyze complex biological datasets and build a more complete portrait of biological systems.
I aim to keep building computational tools and conducting research that benefits both scientists and patients.

// focus


MULTI-OMICS
MICROBIOME
BIOINFORMATICS
MACHINE LEARNING
BIOTECHNOLOGY


// scope





SOP mechanisms and memory of stress response in microbial communities
Environmental stressors induce rapid transcriptional, metabolic, and compositional shifts that can leave short-term ecological memory within microbial communities. I aim to integrate multi-omic methodologies to quantify which functional products appear, how long they persist, and over what timescales communities transition through disturbance and recovery. A central question is whether these responses are predictable and generalizable across different biological systems and perturbations.



Linking phylogeny to stress
Stress events often reshape the genomic context of microbial populations, altering the microbial composition and distribution of mobile elements, accessory genes, and horizontally transferred loci that mediate adaptation. I am interested in how these genomic features reorganize functional potential during environmental pulses, and how lineage-specific traits map onto chemical and metabolite pulses.



Bioprospecting following stress response
A tertiary aim is to discover and characterize novel genes, enzymes, and small molecules that confer stress tolerance or facilitate rapid recovery in their host or ecosystem. Through comparative multi-omics, I will identify candidate loci and products enriched during stress pulses and experimentally validate them using heterologous expression and quantitative assays. This integrative pipeline bridges discovery and application, linking ecological resilience to synthetic biology, natural product discovery, bioremediation, and biotechnological innovation.































// timeline





2024 — Present

Graduate Researcher · Qiyun Lab, Arizona State University
Doctoral research in computational biology, microbiome multi-omics, bioinformatics, and machine-learning methods.




2024 — 2026

M.S. Computational Life Sciences · Arizona State University
Statistical modeling, bioinformatics, and machine learning across genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and other omics data.




2023 — 2025

Quality Control Specialist → Research Associate II · Roche
Routine release testing of multiplexed syndromic diagnostic panels. Designed and executed experiments to expand molecular diagnostic assay offerings.




2021 — 2022

Quality Control Associate I · TriLink Biotechnologies
Release testing of RNA capping reagents and small molecules.




2018 — 2022

B.S. Biological Sciences · California State University, San Marcos

title: MiCBuS — Ovarian HGSOC
text: A lightweight R Markdown / Quarto tutorial probing whether MiCBuS can recover marker genes for a missing ovarian tumor microenvironment population — CAF/stromal cells by default — when that population is present in bulk tumors but omitted from the single-cell reference.

title: MiCBuS — Ovarian HGSOC
text:
What’s inside

Public processed data only — GSE211956 scRNA-seq, TCGA-OV bulk expression, and a couple of Visium spatial sections. No raw reads, no dbGaP/EGA, no CNA validation.
Reduced reference build — broad cell classes (malignant epithelial, stromal/CAF, myeloid, T/NK, B/plasma, endothelial), downsampled per class.
Hold-out experiment — pull CAF/stromal out of the single-cell reference; run MiCBuS against TCGA-OV bulk; check whether COL1A1, COL1A2, DCN, LUM, FAP, ACTA2, PDGFRA, PDGFRB re-emerge in the recovered psMarkers.
Triple validation — held-out scRNA dot/heatmap, bulk score variation across TCGA-OV samples, and spatial feature plots on Visium.

V2 targets 2,000 cells/class, 150 TCGA samples, 300 pseudo-bulk replicates, and two Visium sections.

title: Hantavirus Assay Design
text: An educational portfolio notebook using the 2026 MV Hondius Andes virus cluster as a case study for infectious-disease assay-development reasoning. The project connects virology, epidemiology, phylogenetics, in-silico assay review, and validation planning without providing deployable primer/probe sequences or bench protocols.

title: Hantavirus Assay Design
text:
What’s inside

Current-event framing - public-health context for the MV Hondius Andes virus cluster.
Virology and epidemiology - hantavirus taxonomy, genome structure, host ecology, and transmission considerations.
Assay-design workflow - conceptual TaqMan-style RT-qPCR target-selection, inclusivity, exclusivity, and change-control criteria.
Validation plan - analytical-risk questions that would need to be answered before any laboratory or clinical use.

title: Hantavirus Assay Design
text:
Links

Rendered report - full HTML case study.
Notebook source - Quarto source.
Jupyter notebook - notebook export.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Important



This notebook is an educational molecular diagnostics case study. It describes assay-development reasoning, in-silico review criteria, and validation planning at a conceptual level. It does not provide deployable primer/probe sequences, bench protocols, reaction conditions, clinical decision rules, or instructions for testing patient specimens.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Executive Summary
In May 2026, public health agencies reported a cluster of hantavirus disease linked to the Dutch-flagged cruise ship MV Hondius. WHO reported that the cluster involved Andes virus (ANDV), an American orthohantavirus associated with hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome and unusual among hantaviruses because limited person-to-person transmission has been documented under close-contact conditions. CDC and ECDC both assessed broad public risk as very low while contacts were traced, monitored, and managed through public health channels.
This portfolio notebook approaches the event as a hypothetical diagnostic development exercise:

Frame the biological problem: hantavirus taxonomy, genome structure, host ecology, and clinical syndromes.
Build an epidemiological rationale for molecular detection in a travel-linked outbreak.
Design an in-silico workflow for a TaqMan-style RT-qPCR assay against Andes virus.
Define inclusivity, exclusivity, analytical-risk, and change-control questions an academic or industry team would need to answer before any laboratory validation.

The goal is to demonstrate breadth across virology, epidemiology, phylogenetics, bioinformatics, and molecular diagnostics without presenting an operational test.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this notebook, a reader should be able to:

Explain why hantavirus diagnostics must account for viral diversity, host range, geography, and segmented RNA genome structure.
Distinguish inclusivity from exclusivity testing in infectious disease assay development.
Describe how an in-silico primer/probe design screen can reduce analytical risk before laboratory work.
Build a defensible validation plan for a hypothetical TaqMan assay without claiming clinical performance.
Identify key limitations of sequence-only analyses for diagnostic development.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
section: Current Event: MV Hondius Andes Virus Cluster
text:
Current Event: MV Hondius Andes Virus Cluster
The 2026 outbreak provides a realistic case study because it combines a rare pathogen, international travel, contact tracing, and rapid sequence analysis.
As of WHO’s 13 May 2026 Disease Outbreak News update, 11 cases and three deaths had been reported among people associated with MV Hondius; eight cases were laboratory-confirmed for Andes virus, two were probable, and one was inconclusive pending further testing. WHO assessed global population risk as low while international contact tracing continued. ECDC’s 21 May 2026 update reported 11 total cases, including nine confirmed and two probable cases, and stated that the EU/EEA general-population risk remained very low. CDC described the event as a deadly Andes virus outbreak among passengers and crew of a cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean and stated that the risk of a pandemic and the overall risk to the American public and travelers were extremely low.
Key points for diagnostics:

Outbreak setting: a ship creates repeated close-contact opportunities and complicated exposure histories.
Etiology: Andes virus was identified, not a generic hantavirus.
Transmission concern: Andes virus is exceptional among hantaviruses because limited human-to-human transmission can occur in close-contact contexts.
Sequence data: preliminary analysis of cruise-ship-associated Orthohantavirus andesense virus sequences was posted publicly during the response, illustrating how genomic epidemiology can guide assay review.
Public health posture: risk to the general population remained low, but exposed passengers and crew required active monitoring and coordinated laboratory support.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Hantavirus Biology in One Page
Hantaviruses are negative-sense RNA viruses in the order Bunyavirales. Human disease is associated with rodent-borne orthohantaviruses. The International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses places disease-associated hantaviruses in the family Hantaviridae, subfamily Mammantavirinae, genus Orthohantavirus.
Orthohantaviruses have three genome segments:

S segment: encodes nucleoprotein N, the abundant structural protein that encapsidates viral RNA.
M segment: encodes the glycoprotein precursor, cleaved into Gn and Gc, which mediate entry.
L segment: encodes the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

From a diagnostic target-selection perspective:

The S segment is often attractive because nucleoprotein-coding regions can be relatively conserved within a virus species and abundant transcripts may aid detection.
The M segment can provide lineage information but may show greater diversity in glycoprotein regions under host or immune selection.
The L segment contains conserved polymerase motifs that may support broad detection but can be less discriminating among related viruses.
Segmented genomes create reassortment risk, so a robust assay strategy may use more than one target if a clinical or public-health decision depends on lineage certainty.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Epidemiology and Host Ecology
Hantavirus risk is shaped by host ecology. Human infection classically follows inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secretions from infected rodents or direct rodent contact. Geographic patterns reflect the distribution of reservoir hosts.
Broad clinical-epidemiological groupings are:









Group
Typical geography
Examples
Main syndrome association




Old World orthohantaviruses
Europe and Asia
Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, Seoul
Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome


New World orthohantaviruses
Americas
Andes, Sin Nombre
Hantavirus pulmonary/cardiopulmonary syndrome



For the MV Hondius case study, the key epidemiological feature is not that hantaviruses suddenly became broadly transmissible. Rather, Andes virus has a documented history of limited person-to-person transmission, and a cruise ship is a closed travel environment where contact networks, shared cabins, caregiving, medical evacuation, and international disembarkation can complicate response.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
section: Phylogeny: What the Tree Should Answer
text:
Phylogeny: What the Tree Should Answer
Phylogenetics should serve an assay-development question, not just produce a tree. For a hypothetical Andes virus TaqMan assay, the useful questions are:

Target scope: Should the assay detect all known Andes virus diversity, a recent outbreak lineage, or a broader American hantavirus group?
Near-neighbor risk: Which related orthohantaviruses are close enough to create cross-reactivity concerns?
Sequence coverage: Are available genomes representative of geography, time, host, and clinical cases?
Segment consistency: Do S, M, and L trees tell the same story, or is reassortment or incomplete sampling plausible?
Outbreak stability: Do the outbreak sequences introduce mismatches in any candidate binding region?



Code
flowchart LR
  A[Public sequence set] --> B[Segment-aware curation]
  B --> C[Multiple sequence alignment]
  C --> D[Phylogenetic tree per segment]
  C --> E[Conservation scan]
  D --> F[Target scope decision]
  E --> G[Candidate assay windows]
  F --> H[Inclusivity panel]
  G --> I[Primer/probe candidate ranking]
  H --> J[In-silico inclusivity]
  I --> J
  I --> K[In-silico exclusivity]












Example Phylogenetic Framing
For a portfolio analysis, I would include reference sequences from:

Andes virus lineages from Chile and Argentina, including historical human and reservoir-associated sequences.
Other New World hantaviruses such as Sin Nombre, Laguna Negra, Choclo, Bayou, Black Creek Canal, and related taxa.
Old World representatives such as Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, and Seoul as distant outgroups.
Any public MV Hondius-linked sequences, tagged as outbreak sequences and reviewed separately from the design training set.

The tree should not be overinterpreted as transmission proof. It is mainly used to understand diversity, confirm that target panels span known variation, and identify near-neighbor taxa for exclusivity testing.
# Illustrative only. Do not run as a diagnostic workflow.
# A real analysis would require curated accessions, versioned FASTA files,
# documented filters, and reproducible alignment/tree software.

candidate_segments = ["S", "M", "L"]

reference_panel = {
    "target": ["Andes virus sequences across geography and collection years"],
    "near_neighbors": ["other American orthohantaviruses"],
    "distant_outgroups": ["Old World orthohantaviruses"],
    "outbreak_review": ["public MV Hondius-associated sequences, if available"],
}

for segment in candidate_segments:
    print(f"Curate {segment} segment sequences")
    print("Align sequences, mask low-quality regions, build tree")
    print("Scan conserved candidate windows and annotate mismatches")

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Diagnostic Design Goal
The hypothetical product requirement is:

Develop a TaqMan-style one-step RT-qPCR screening assay intended to detect Andes virus RNA in appropriately handled clinical or public-health specimens, with design evidence supporting broad inclusivity across known Andes virus diversity and exclusivity against near-neighbor hantaviruses, common respiratory pathogens, host sequences, and environmental/background organisms.

This is a design brief, not a validated intended-use statement. A real clinical assay would require regulatory review, biosafety assessment, specimen-type validation, analytical and clinical performance studies, controls, quality systems, and laboratory authorization.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Why TaqMan RT-qPCR?
A hydrolysis-probe RT-qPCR format is commonly used in molecular diagnostics because it can combine:

sequence-specific amplification and sequence-specific probe detection,
real-time amplification monitoring,
compatibility with multiplex internal controls,
straightforward analytical validation metrics such as limit of detection, precision, interference, inclusivity, and cross-reactivity.

For an RNA virus, reverse transcription must precede amplification. From a diagnostic-development perspective, the design risk is not just whether a primer pair can amplify a reference sequence; it is whether the assay remains robust across target diversity and does not produce misleading positives from non-target templates.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Target Selection Strategy
Candidate targets should be evaluated across the three segments:









Criterion
S segment
M segment
L segment




Within-Andes conservation
Often favorable
Variable by region
Conserved motifs possible


Discrimination from near neighbors
Moderate to high
Often high
May be lower in conserved motifs


Phylogenetic information
Good for lineage context
Good for lineage/context
Good for deeper relationships


Diagnostic risk
Misses if local variation affects binding
Misses if highly variable
Cross-reactivity if too conserved



Pragmatic strategy:

Start with conserved windows in S and L.
Reject windows with known outbreak-sequence mismatches near primer 3-prime ends or probe-critical positions.
Prefer windows that are conserved across target diversity but separated from near-neighbor hantaviruses by multiple informative differences.
Consider a two-target assay if single-target dropout risk is unacceptable.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
In-Silico Primer/Probe Design Constraints
The following are design-review constraints, not a complete recipe:







Feature
Review expectation




Amplicon
Short enough for degraded RNA and efficient qPCR, while retaining specificity


Primers
Similar melting behavior, limited self-complementarity, limited primer-dimer risk


Probe
Binds within a conserved, target-informative region and avoids highly variable positions


3-prime ends
Avoid target polymorphisms and avoid strong non-target complementarity


GC content
Avoid extremes that impair binding, amplification, or synthesis


Degeneracy
Use sparingly; degeneracy can improve inclusivity but reduce sensitivity and specificity


Multiplexing
Check interaction among all primers, probes, and internal controls


Sequence drift
Establish a surveillance process for new public genomes



No candidate oligonucleotide sequences are shown here. For a portfolio piece, the strongest demonstration is a transparent decision framework, ranked candidate windows, and a validation plan rather than a deployable assay.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Inclusivity Testing
Inclusivity asks whether target strains or target nucleic acids are expected to test positive. In silico, the panel should cover the intended target breadth.
For an Andes virus assay, the inclusivity panel should include:

geographically diverse Andes virus sequences,
human and reservoir-associated sequences where available,
historical and recent sequences,
all available segments relevant to the chosen target,
outbreak-associated sequences treated as a challenge set,
partial sequences only when the candidate target region is covered and quality is sufficient.

In-silico inclusivity outputs should include:

number of target sequences evaluated,
number with full amplicon coverage,
number with perfect primer and probe matches,
mismatch count by oligo and position,
special flag for mismatches near primer 3-prime ends,
estimated dropout risk by lineage or geography,
a list of sequences excluded because of incomplete coverage or poor quality.

# Mock schema for an inclusivity review table.
# Values below are placeholders for notebook demonstration only.

import pandas as pd

inclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "sequence_id": "ANDV_reference_or_lineage_id",
            "segment": "S",
            "region": "candidate_window_A",
            "forward_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "probe_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "reverse_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
            "rationale": "e.g., no critical 3-prime mismatch",
        }
    ]
)

inclusivity_schema

Inclusivity Decision Rules
An assay-development team should define decision rules before ranking candidates. Example rules:

Pass: all target sequences with adequate coverage have no critical primer/probe mismatches.
Review: one or more target sequences have mismatches outside critical positions, or coverage gaps make a lineage hard to assess.
Fail: a known target lineage or outbreak sequence has a mismatch pattern expected to reduce amplification or probe detection.

These rules should be documented and version controlled. The decision should not be adjusted after seeing inconvenient results unless the change is justified and recorded.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Exclusivity Testing
Exclusivity asks whether non-target organisms or nucleic acids are expected to test negative. For a molecular assay, the panel should include both phylogenetic near neighbors and realistic sample-background organisms.
For Andes virus, exclusivity should include:

non-Andes New World hantaviruses,
Old World hantaviruses,
common respiratory viruses that may be present in respiratory specimens,
human genome and transcriptome sequences,
rodent host sequences if environmental or reservoir samples are in scope,
bacterial and fungal organisms common in respiratory or environmental backgrounds, if those matrices are claimed.

In-silico exclusivity should not rely only on short local matches. The critical question is whether a primer pair can plausibly amplify a product of the expected size and whether the probe can bind that product. NCBI Primer-BLAST is useful because it combines primer design with specificity checking; NCBI advises selecting the relevant organism and smallest appropriate database for precise checks, or broader databases when broad coverage is needed.
# Mock schema for an exclusivity screen.

exclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "non_target_group": "Sin Nombre virus",
            "database": "curated_hantavirus_near_neighbor_set",
            "forward_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "reverse_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "probe_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "predicted_amplicon": "none / out_of_range / plausible",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
        }
    ]
)

exclusivity_schema

Cross-Reactivity Risk Matrix









Risk tier
Example non-targets
Why they matter
Expected evidence




Tier 1
Closely related American hantaviruses
Highest sequence similarity
Curated local alignment and in-silico PCR


Tier 2
Old World hantaviruses
Same genus, different disease ecology
Broad BLAST or curated database search


Tier 3
Human and host background
Sample matrix contamination
Human/host genome and transcriptome screen


Tier 4
Respiratory co-pathogens
Clinical differential diagnosis
Panel review based on intended specimen type


Tier 5
Environmental organisms
If environmental matrices are claimed
Matrix-specific database screen

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Candidate Ranking Framework
A useful portfolio artifact is a scored design table. The values below are placeholders; in a real project they would be computed from curated alignments and specificity searches.
candidate_scores = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "candidate": "S_window_A",
            "target_segment": "S",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.98,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.95,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.90,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 1,
            "decision": "advance to non-clinical feasibility review",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "L_window_B",
            "target_segment": "L",
            "inclusivity_score": 1.00,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.82,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.88,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 2,
            "decision": "hold as backup target; review near-neighbor risk",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "M_window_C",
            "target_segment": "M",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.86,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.97,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.92,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 0.90,
            "overall_rank": 3,
            "decision": "do not advance without redesign",
        },
    ]
)

candidate_scores
Suggested ranking components:

Target conservation: fraction of target sequences without critical mismatches.
Near-neighbor separation: absence of plausible non-target amplicons.
Oligo physical quality: absence of strong self-complementarity, primer dimers, extreme GC, or problematic runs.
Outbreak robustness: no critical mismatch in current outbreak sequences.
Manufacturability: avoids unusual modifications unless justified.
Change-control burden: target region is stable enough for surveillance.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Analytical Validation Plan
After in-silico review, a real molecular diagnostic assay would need laboratory validation. This notebook does not provide protocols, but the validation plan would normally address:







Performance characteristic
Question




Limit of detection
What is the lowest concentration detected with acceptable probability in each claimed specimen type?


Inclusivity
Does the assay detect representative target diversity?


Exclusivity / cross-reactivity
Does the assay avoid detection of near neighbors and likely co-occurring organisms?


Precision
Are results reproducible across operators, instruments, reagent lots, days, and sites?


Interference
Do substances or specimen matrix effects inhibit detection?


Stability
Are specimens, controls, and reagents stable under claimed conditions?


Controls
Do extraction, amplification, inhibition, positive, and negative controls behave as expected?


Clinical evaluation
Does assay performance support the intended use in the target population?



CLSI MM03 describes molecular diagnostic methods for infectious diseases, including sequence selection, performance evaluation, inhibitors, false-positive control, interpretation, quality assurance, and regulatory considerations. CLSI MM17 addresses validation and verification concepts for qualitative multiplex nucleic acid assays. Those standards are useful anchors for turning an in-silico design into a regulated diagnostic-development plan.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Regulatory and Quality Considerations
An industry or clinical laboratory team should separate four claims:

In-silico design claim: the target window is predicted to be inclusive and exclusive based on the current sequence database.
Analytical performance claim: the assay detects target material under controlled laboratory conditions.
Clinical performance claim: the assay identifies infected patients in the intended population and specimen type.
Public health utility claim: the assay supports outbreak response, triage, surveillance, or contact investigation.

Only the first claim can be supported by this notebook. The other claims require laboratory and clinical evidence.
Version control should capture:

source databases and access dates,
accession lists and exclusion criteria,
alignment and tree software versions,
primer/probe design software and parameters,
candidate scoring rules,
review sign-off,
periodic re-analysis triggers when new sequences appear.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Failure Modes and Mitigations








Failure mode
How it could happen
Mitigation




False negative from sequence drift
Mutation in primer/probe binding site
Multi-sequence inclusivity screen and periodic surveillance


False negative from incomplete sampling
Missing lineage absent from databases
Conservative target choice and documented uncertainty


False positive from near neighbor
Related hantavirus amplified
Near-neighbor exclusivity panel and in-silico PCR


False positive from contamination
Amplicon or control contamination
Workflow controls and physical separation in real labs


Inhibition
Specimen matrix suppresses amplification
Internal control and specimen validation


Overclaiming
In-silico pass treated as clinical validation
Clear claim boundaries and regulatory review

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Discussion
The MV Hondius cluster is a strong case study because it shows how modern molecular diagnostics sits between public health and molecular biology. Phylogeny helps define the target and near-neighbor space. Epidemiology defines who might be tested, what specimens might be relevant, and what turnaround time would matter. Assay design translates sequence conservation into a practical screen, while inclusivity and exclusivity testing force the developer to state what the assay is, and is not, designed to detect.
The most important scientific discipline in this exercise is restraint. A sequence-perfect candidate is not a clinical test. A beautiful phylogenetic tree does not prove transmission. A negative in-silico cross-reactivity search does not replace wet-lab validation. For molecular diagnostics, evidence must be layered: genomic rationale first, analytical performance second, clinical performance third, and public-health utility last.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Reproducibility Checklist

Define intended target scope: Andes virus only, outbreak lineage, or broad hantavirus screen.
Freeze accession lists and database versions.
Curate sequences by segment, quality, geography, host, and date.
Align S, M, and L segments separately.
Build segment-specific phylogenies.
Identify conserved target windows.
Rank primer/probe candidates without publishing operational sequences.
Run in-silico inclusivity against target diversity.
Run in-silico exclusivity against near neighbors and background organisms.
Document limitations and candidate-selection rationale.
Define the laboratory validation plan without claiming validation.
Add periodic sequence-surveillance triggers.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Sources

CDC. Andes Virus Outbreak on a Cruise Ship: Current Situation. Accessed 22 May 2026.
CDC Health Alert Network. 2026 Multi-country Hantavirus Cluster Linked to Cruise Ship. 8 May 2026.
ECDC. Andes hantavirus outbreak in cruise ship, 21 May 2026.
WHO. Hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel, Multi-country. Disease Outbreak News, 13 May 2026.
Virological. Preliminary analysis of Orthohantavirus andesense virus sequences from a cruise-ship related cluster, May 2026.
ICTV. Genus Orthohantavirus.
NCBI. Design PCR primers and check them for specificity.
CLSI. MM03: Molecular Diagnostic Methods for Infectious Diseases.
CLSI. MM17: Validation and Verification of Multiplex Nucleic Acid Assays.
ATCC. Infectious Disease Assay Development: Establishing Inclusivity Exclusivity.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text: skid·əl · A Colab-runnable walkthrough that takes raw long reads from an environmental sample all the way to manually-binned genomes — explaining every step.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
TL;DR

Input: Oxford Nanopore reads from a known mock community
Pipeline: raw reads → QC → Flye --meta assembly → composition + coverage features → interactive binning in BinaRena
Output: a handful of (hopefully separable) Metagenome-Assembled Genomes, plus an understanding of why every step is there

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
Who this is for
Practitioners — a copy-pasteable scaffold for a small long-read metagenomics run.
Learners — every section pairs a concept with a runnable example. The algorithm detour (§7) shows what assemblers are actually doing under the hood, in <50 lines each.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
1. The biological question
Metagenomics: sequencing the collective DNA of a microbial community without isolating individual organisms.
Shotgun metagenomics fragments all DNA randomly, sequences the fragments, then tries to reconstruct each organism’s genome from the resulting jigsaw. The reconstructed pieces are called contigs; grouping contigs that came from the same organism is binning; a confident bin is a MAG (Metagenome-Assembled Genome).

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
2. Why long reads?
Short reads (Illumina) are accurate per-base but trip on repeats — any genomic region longer than the read collapses into ambiguous graph nodes. Microbial genomes (and especially metagenomes, where conserved regions are shared across species) are full of them.
Long reads (Oxford Nanopore, PacBio) span repeats. The historical trade-off was per-base error — modern ONT R10 + recent basecallers have closed most of that gap.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
3. The dataset
We’ll use a subset of the ZymoBIOMICS D6300 mock community — a defined mix of 8 bacteria + 2 yeasts. Known ground truth means we can evaluate whether our binning actually worked.
Starting point (verify it fits Colab limits — downsample with seqtk sample if needed):

SRR8351023 — Zymo GridION run (Nicholls et al. 2019)

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
4. Environment setup
Colab gives us a fresh VM; condacolab lets us install bioconda tooling without fighting pip.

!pip install -q condacolab
import condacolab
condacolab.install()
# Kernel restarts after this — expected.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
5. Fetching reads from the SRA
NCBI’s SRA stores raw sequencing reads. sra-tools gives us prefetch (download .sra) and fasterq-dump (extract FASTQ).

!conda install -y -c bioconda sra-tools

ACCESSION = "SRR8351023"  # confirm / swap for your chosen Zymo subset
!prefetch {ACCESSION}
!fasterq-dump --threads 4 {ACCESSION}

# Optional: downsample so Flye finishes inside Colab's wall-clock budget
# !conda install -y -c bioconda seqtk
# !seqtk sample -s42 {ACCESSION}.fastq 0.1 > reads.sub.fastq

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
6. Read QC (before you assemble anything)
Always look at your reads before assembling them. For ONT data, three things to check:

Read length distribution — long enough to be useful?
Quality (Phred / Q-score) distribution — basecaller version matters
Throughput — enough coverage to assemble?


!pip install -q NanoPlot
!NanoPlot --fastq {ACCESSION}.fastq -o nanoplot_out --tsv_stats

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
8. Assembly with Flye --meta
--meta tells Flye not to assume uniform coverage — in a community, different species are present at very different abundances and a uniform-coverage prior would discard the rare ones as errors.

!conda install -y -c bioconda flye


!flye --help


# Note: --genome-size is OPTIONAL with --meta; omit if you don't know the total size.
!flye \
  --nano-raw {ACCESSION}.fastq \
  --out-dir out_assembly \
  --meta \
  --threads 4


8a. Inspecting the assembly
Flye produces:

assembly.fasta — the contigs
assembly_info.txt — per-contig stats (length, coverage, circular?)
assembly_graph.gfa — the repeat graph
flye.log — overall stats including N50, mean divergence


!tail -40 out_assembly/flye.log
print("---")
!head out_assembly/assembly_info.txt

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
9. Viewing the assembly graph
assembly_graph.gfa is best viewed in Bandage (or AGB / IGV). Bandage runs locally, so this is the one step that doesn’t live in Colab.
The interesting things to annotate on the graph view are the circular contigs (likely complete chromosomes — a win for long reads) versus the tangled regions where the assembler couldn’t resolve repeats.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
section: 10. From contigs to bins — the intuition
text:
10. From contigs to bins — the intuition
After assembly we have a bag of contigs with no labels. To group them by source organism we use two signals that tend to be conserved within a genome but vary across genomes:

Composition — every genome has a characteristic k-mer (tetra-/pentanucleotide) signature.
Coverage — contigs from the same organism appear at the same abundance, so they recruit similar read depth.

Plot contigs in (composition, coverage) space and same-organism contigs cluster together. That’s the entire conceptual basis for modern binning — every tool from MetaBAT2 to SemiBin2 is some variant of this picture.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
11. Setting up BinaRena
BinaRena is a browser-based tool for manual, interactive binning. Its companion scripts compute the composition + coverage features we need.

!conda install -y -c conda-forge -c bioconda \
  scikit-bio scikit-learn umap-learn \
  minimap2 samtools \
  checkm2


!wget -q https://github.com/qiyunlab/binarena/archive/refs/heads/master.zip
!unzip -j -o master.zip 'binarena-master/scripts/*.py' -d scripts
!chmod +x scripts/*.py


!ln -sf out_assembly/assembly.fasta contigs.fna


11a. Composition features (k-mers)
Count 5-mers per contig, then project to 2D with PCA / t-SNE / UMAP so we can see cluster structure.

!scripts/sequence_basics.py -i contigs.fna -o basic.tsv
!head basic.tsv


!scripts/count_kmers.py -i contigs.fna -k 5 | \
  scripts/reduce_dimension.py --pca --tsne --umap -o k5



11b. Coverage features
Map the original reads back to the assembled contigs. Contigs from the same genome get hit at roughly the same depth.
Note: for ONT/PacBio long reads use minimap2 -ax map-ont, not bowtie2 — bowtie2 is a short-read aligner and will mis-handle long, noisy reads.

!minimap2 -ax map-ont -t 4 contigs.fna {ACCESSION}.fastq | \
  samtools sort -@ 4 -o reads.bam
!samtools index reads.bam


!samtools coverage reads.bam > coverage.tsv
!head coverage.tsv

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
12. Interactive binning in BinaRena
Open binarena-master/BinaRena.html locally in a browser, then load:

basic.tsv — length, GC
k5.tsv — composition coordinates (PCA / t-SNE / UMAP)
coverage.tsv — per-contig depth

Plot composition-1 vs composition-2, color by coverage, size by contig length. Lasso each cluster → name → export as a bin.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
13. Limitations & honest caveats

Colab. Real environmental metagenomes need 64+ GB RAM and hours of CPU. This pipeline is built around a small mock community on purpose.
Manual binning doesn’t scale. It’s a teaching tool; for real datasets you’d use MetaBAT2 / SemiBin2 / vamb and fall back to BinaRena for inspection and curating edge cases.
No polishing step. ONT assemblies usually want a polishing pass (Medaka, Racon) before downstream analysis — skipped here for brevity.
No taxonomy. We separated organisms but didn’t name them. Add GTDB-Tk or Kraken2 if you need labels.

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
section: 14. What you’d reach for at scale
text:
14. What you’d reach for at scale








stage
demo here
production




QC
NanoPlot
NanoPlot + Filtlong / chopper


Assembly
Flye --meta
Flye + metaMDBG; polish with Medaka


Binning
BinaRena (manual)
MetaBAT2 / SemiBin2 / vamb, ensembled w/ DAS_Tool


Validation
(omitted)
CheckM2, GTDB-Tk


Read mapping
minimap2
minimap2 (same)

title: SKIDLE — Long-read metagenomics notebook (source)
text:
15. Further reading

Kolmogorov et al. 2020 — metaFlye: scalable long-read metagenome assembly
Nicholls et al. 2019 — Ultra-deep, long-read nanopore sequencing of mock microbial community standards (the Zymo dataset paper)
BinaRena: https://github.com/qiyunlab/binarena
Anvi’o tutorials for a richer view of MAG curation

title: SKIDLE
text:
What’s inside

Mock-community anchor — ZymoBIOMICS D6300 ONT reads from the SRA, sized to fit Colab’s RAM/wall-clock budget.
Assembly path — read QC with NanoPlot → Flye --meta assembly → assembly-graph inspection in Bandage.
Algorithm detour — minimal Python implementations of a De Bruijn graph, overlap-layout-consensus, and a suffix trie, to demystify what assemblers and mappers are actually doing.
Binning from first principles — k-mer composition (PCA / t-SNE / UMAP) plus long-read coverage (minimap2 + samtools), then manual binning in BinaRena.
Honest limits — Colab budget, no polishing or taxonomy, and what you’d reach for at scale (MetaBAT2 / SemiBin2 / vamb / CheckM2).

title: SKIDLE
text:
Links

Rendered notebook — full walkthrough as a static page.
Notebook source (.qmd) — Quarto-ported markdown source.
Download .ipynb — raw Jupyter notebook to run locally.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
Important



This notebook is an educational molecular diagnostics case study. It describes assay-development reasoning, in-silico review criteria, and validation planning at a conceptual level. It does not provide deployable primer/probe sequences, bench protocols, reaction conditions, clinical decision rules, or instructions for testing patient specimens.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
1 Executive Summary
In May 2026, public health agencies reported a cluster of hantavirus disease linked to the Dutch-flagged cruise ship MV Hondius. WHO reported that the cluster involved Andes virus (ANDV), an American orthohantavirus associated with hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome and unusual among hantaviruses because limited person-to-person transmission has been documented under close-contact conditions. CDC and ECDC both assessed broad public risk as very low while contacts were traced, monitored, and managed through public health channels.
This portfolio notebook approaches the event as a hypothetical diagnostic development exercise:

Frame the biological problem: hantavirus taxonomy, genome structure, host ecology, and clinical syndromes.
Build an epidemiological rationale for molecular detection in a travel-linked outbreak.
Design an in-silico workflow for a TaqMan-style RT-qPCR assay against Andes virus.
Define inclusivity, exclusivity, analytical-risk, and change-control questions an academic or industry team would need to answer before any laboratory validation.

The goal is to demonstrate breadth across virology, epidemiology, phylogenetics, bioinformatics, and molecular diagnostics without presenting an operational test.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this notebook, a reader should be able to:

Explain why hantavirus diagnostics must account for viral diversity, host range, geography, and segmented RNA genome structure.
Distinguish inclusivity from exclusivity testing in infectious disease assay development.
Describe how an in-silico primer/probe design screen can reduce analytical risk before laboratory work.
Build a defensible validation plan for a hypothetical TaqMan assay without claiming clinical performance.
Identify key limitations of sequence-only analyses for diagnostic development.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
section: 3 Current Event: MV Hondius Andes Virus Cluster
text:
3 Current Event: MV Hondius Andes Virus Cluster
The 2026 outbreak provides a realistic case study because it combines a rare pathogen, international travel, contact tracing, and rapid sequence analysis.
As of WHO’s 13 May 2026 Disease Outbreak News update, 11 cases and three deaths had been reported among people associated with MV Hondius; eight cases were laboratory-confirmed for Andes virus, two were probable, and one was inconclusive pending further testing. WHO assessed global population risk as low while international contact tracing continued. ECDC’s 21 May 2026 update reported 11 total cases, including nine confirmed and two probable cases, and stated that the EU/EEA general-population risk remained very low. CDC described the event as a deadly Andes virus outbreak among passengers and crew of a cruise ship in the Atlantic Ocean and stated that the risk of a pandemic and the overall risk to the American public and travelers were extremely low.
Key points for diagnostics:

Outbreak setting: a ship creates repeated close-contact opportunities and complicated exposure histories.
Etiology: Andes virus was identified, not a generic hantavirus.
Transmission concern: Andes virus is exceptional among hantaviruses because limited human-to-human transmission can occur in close-contact contexts.
Sequence data: preliminary analysis of cruise-ship-associated Orthohantavirus andesense virus sequences was posted publicly during the response, illustrating how genomic epidemiology can guide assay review.
Public health posture: risk to the general population remained low, but exposed passengers and crew required active monitoring and coordinated laboratory support.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
4 Hantavirus Biology in One Page
Hantaviruses are negative-sense RNA viruses in the order Bunyavirales. Human disease is associated with rodent-borne orthohantaviruses. The International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses places disease-associated hantaviruses in the family Hantaviridae, subfamily Mammantavirinae, genus Orthohantavirus.
Orthohantaviruses have three genome segments:

S segment: encodes nucleoprotein N, the abundant structural protein that encapsidates viral RNA.
M segment: encodes the glycoprotein precursor, cleaved into Gn and Gc, which mediate entry.
L segment: encodes the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

From a diagnostic target-selection perspective:

The S segment is often attractive because nucleoprotein-coding regions can be relatively conserved within a virus species and abundant transcripts may aid detection.
The M segment can provide lineage information but may show greater diversity in glycoprotein regions under host or immune selection.
The L segment contains conserved polymerase motifs that may support broad detection but can be less discriminating among related viruses.
Segmented genomes create reassortment risk, so a robust assay strategy may use more than one target if a clinical or public-health decision depends on lineage certainty.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
5 Epidemiology and Host Ecology
Hantavirus risk is shaped by host ecology. Human infection classically follows inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secretions from infected rodents or direct rodent contact. Geographic patterns reflect the distribution of reservoir hosts.
Broad clinical-epidemiological groupings are:









Group
Typical geography
Examples
Main syndrome association




Old World orthohantaviruses
Europe and Asia
Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, Seoul
Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome


New World orthohantaviruses
Americas
Andes, Sin Nombre
Hantavirus pulmonary/cardiopulmonary syndrome



For the MV Hondius case study, the key epidemiological feature is not that hantaviruses suddenly became broadly transmissible. Rather, Andes virus has a documented history of limited person-to-person transmission, and a cruise ship is a closed travel environment where contact networks, shared cabins, caregiving, medical evacuation, and international disembarkation can complicate response.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
section: 6 Phylogeny: What the Tree Should Answer
text:
6 Phylogeny: What the Tree Should Answer
Phylogenetics should serve an assay-development question, not just produce a tree. For a hypothetical Andes virus TaqMan assay, the useful questions are:

Target scope: Should the assay detect all known Andes virus diversity, a recent outbreak lineage, or a broader American hantavirus group?
Near-neighbor risk: Which related orthohantaviruses are close enough to create cross-reactivity concerns?
Sequence coverage: Are available genomes representative of geography, time, host, and clinical cases?
Segment consistency: Do S, M, and L trees tell the same story, or is reassortment or incomplete sampling plausible?
Outbreak stability: Do the outbreak sequences introduce mismatches in any candidate binding region?






flowchart LR
  A[Public sequence set] --> B[Segment-aware curation]
  B --> C[Multiple sequence alignment]
  C --> D[Phylogenetic tree per segment]
  C --> E[Conservation scan]
  D --> F[Target scope decision]
  E --> G[Candidate assay windows]
  F --> H[Inclusivity panel]
  G --> I[Primer/probe candidate ranking]
  H --> J[In-silico inclusivity]
  I --> J
  I --> K[In-silico exclusivity]







6.1 Example Phylogenetic Framing
For a portfolio analysis, I would include reference sequences from:

Andes virus lineages from Chile and Argentina, including historical human and reservoir-associated sequences.
Other New World hantaviruses such as Sin Nombre, Laguna Negra, Choclo, Bayou, Black Creek Canal, and related taxa.
Old World representatives such as Hantaan, Dobrava, Puumala, and Seoul as distant outgroups.
Any public MV Hondius-linked sequences, tagged as outbreak sequences and reviewed separately from the design training set.

The tree should not be overinterpreted as transmission proof. It is mainly used to understand diversity, confirm that target panels span known variation, and identify near-neighbor taxa for exclusivity testing.
# Illustrative only. Do not run as a diagnostic workflow.
# A real analysis would require curated accessions, versioned FASTA files,
# documented filters, and reproducible alignment/tree software.

candidate_segments = ["S", "M", "L"]

reference_panel = {
    "target": ["Andes virus sequences across geography and collection years"],
    "near_neighbors": ["other American orthohantaviruses"],
    "distant_outgroups": ["Old World orthohantaviruses"],
    "outbreak_review": ["public MV Hondius-associated sequences, if available"],
}

for segment in candidate_segments:
    print(f"Curate {segment} segment sequences")
    print("Align sequences, mask low-quality regions, build tree")
    print("Scan conserved candidate windows and annotate mismatches")

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
7 Diagnostic Design Goal
The hypothetical product requirement is:

Develop a TaqMan-style one-step RT-qPCR screening assay intended to detect Andes virus RNA in appropriately handled clinical or public-health specimens, with design evidence supporting broad inclusivity across known Andes virus diversity and exclusivity against near-neighbor hantaviruses, common respiratory pathogens, host sequences, and environmental/background organisms.

This is a design brief, not a validated intended-use statement. A real clinical assay would require regulatory review, biosafety assessment, specimen-type validation, analytical and clinical performance studies, controls, quality systems, and laboratory authorization.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
8 Why TaqMan RT-qPCR?
A hydrolysis-probe RT-qPCR format is commonly used in molecular diagnostics because it can combine:

sequence-specific amplification and sequence-specific probe detection,
real-time amplification monitoring,
compatibility with multiplex internal controls,
straightforward analytical validation metrics such as limit of detection, precision, interference, inclusivity, and cross-reactivity.

For an RNA virus, reverse transcription must precede amplification. From a diagnostic-development perspective, the design risk is not just whether a primer pair can amplify a reference sequence; it is whether the assay remains robust across target diversity and does not produce misleading positives from non-target templates.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
9 Target Selection Strategy
Candidate targets should be evaluated across the three segments:









Criterion
S segment
M segment
L segment




Within-Andes conservation
Often favorable
Variable by region
Conserved motifs possible


Discrimination from near neighbors
Moderate to high
Often high
May be lower in conserved motifs


Phylogenetic information
Good for lineage context
Good for lineage/context
Good for deeper relationships


Diagnostic risk
Misses if local variation affects binding
Misses if highly variable
Cross-reactivity if too conserved



Pragmatic strategy:

Start with conserved windows in S and L.
Reject windows with known outbreak-sequence mismatches near primer 3-prime ends or probe-critical positions.
Prefer windows that are conserved across target diversity but separated from near-neighbor hantaviruses by multiple informative differences.
Consider a two-target assay if single-target dropout risk is unacceptable.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
10 In-Silico Primer/Probe Design Constraints
The following are design-review constraints, not a complete recipe:







Feature
Review expectation




Amplicon
Short enough for degraded RNA and efficient qPCR, while retaining specificity


Primers
Similar melting behavior, limited self-complementarity, limited primer-dimer risk


Probe
Binds within a conserved, target-informative region and avoids highly variable positions


3-prime ends
Avoid target polymorphisms and avoid strong non-target complementarity


GC content
Avoid extremes that impair binding, amplification, or synthesis


Degeneracy
Use sparingly; degeneracy can improve inclusivity but reduce sensitivity and specificity


Multiplexing
Check interaction among all primers, probes, and internal controls


Sequence drift
Establish a surveillance process for new public genomes



No candidate oligonucleotide sequences are shown here. For a portfolio piece, the strongest demonstration is a transparent decision framework, ranked candidate windows, and a validation plan rather than a deployable assay.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
11 Inclusivity Testing
Inclusivity asks whether target strains or target nucleic acids are expected to test positive. In silico, the panel should cover the intended target breadth.
For an Andes virus assay, the inclusivity panel should include:

geographically diverse Andes virus sequences,
human and reservoir-associated sequences where available,
historical and recent sequences,
all available segments relevant to the chosen target,
outbreak-associated sequences treated as a challenge set,
partial sequences only when the candidate target region is covered and quality is sufficient.

In-silico inclusivity outputs should include:

number of target sequences evaluated,
number with full amplicon coverage,
number with perfect primer and probe matches,
mismatch count by oligo and position,
special flag for mismatches near primer 3-prime ends,
estimated dropout risk by lineage or geography,
a list of sequences excluded because of incomplete coverage or poor quality.

# Mock schema for an inclusivity review table.
# Values below are placeholders for notebook demonstration only.

import pandas as pd

inclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "sequence_id": "ANDV_reference_or_lineage_id",
            "segment": "S",
            "region": "candidate_window_A",
            "forward_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "probe_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "reverse_mismatches": "count_and_positions",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
            "rationale": "e.g., no critical 3-prime mismatch",
        }
    ]
)

inclusivity_schema

11.1 Inclusivity Decision Rules
An assay-development team should define decision rules before ranking candidates. Example rules:

Pass: all target sequences with adequate coverage have no critical primer/probe mismatches.
Review: one or more target sequences have mismatches outside critical positions, or coverage gaps make a lineage hard to assess.
Fail: a known target lineage or outbreak sequence has a mismatch pattern expected to reduce amplification or probe detection.

These rules should be documented and version controlled. The decision should not be adjusted after seeing inconvenient results unless the change is justified and recorded.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
12 Exclusivity Testing
Exclusivity asks whether non-target organisms or nucleic acids are expected to test negative. For a molecular assay, the panel should include both phylogenetic near neighbors and realistic sample-background organisms.
For Andes virus, exclusivity should include:

non-Andes New World hantaviruses,
Old World hantaviruses,
common respiratory viruses that may be present in respiratory specimens,
human genome and transcriptome sequences,
rodent host sequences if environmental or reservoir samples are in scope,
bacterial and fungal organisms common in respiratory or environmental backgrounds, if those matrices are claimed.

In-silico exclusivity should not rely only on short local matches. The critical question is whether a primer pair can plausibly amplify a product of the expected size and whether the probe can bind that product. NCBI Primer-BLAST is useful because it combines primer design with specificity checking; NCBI advises selecting the relevant organism and smallest appropriate database for precise checks, or broader databases when broad coverage is needed.
# Mock schema for an exclusivity screen.

exclusivity_schema = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "non_target_group": "Sin Nombre virus",
            "database": "curated_hantavirus_near_neighbor_set",
            "forward_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "reverse_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "probe_hit": "yes/no + mismatch summary",
            "predicted_amplicon": "none / out_of_range / plausible",
            "risk_call": "pass / review / fail",
        }
    ]
)

exclusivity_schema

12.1 Cross-Reactivity Risk Matrix









Risk tier
Example non-targets
Why they matter
Expected evidence




Tier 1
Closely related American hantaviruses
Highest sequence similarity
Curated local alignment and in-silico PCR


Tier 2
Old World hantaviruses
Same genus, different disease ecology
Broad BLAST or curated database search


Tier 3
Human and host background
Sample matrix contamination
Human/host genome and transcriptome screen


Tier 4
Respiratory co-pathogens
Clinical differential diagnosis
Panel review based on intended specimen type


Tier 5
Environmental organisms
If environmental matrices are claimed
Matrix-specific database screen

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
13 Candidate Ranking Framework
A useful portfolio artifact is a scored design table. The values below are placeholders; in a real project they would be computed from curated alignments and specificity searches.
candidate_scores = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "candidate": "S_window_A",
            "target_segment": "S",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.98,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.95,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.90,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 1,
            "decision": "advance to non-clinical feasibility review",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "L_window_B",
            "target_segment": "L",
            "inclusivity_score": 1.00,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.82,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.88,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 1.00,
            "overall_rank": 2,
            "decision": "hold as backup target; review near-neighbor risk",
        },
        {
            "candidate": "M_window_C",
            "target_segment": "M",
            "inclusivity_score": 0.86,
            "exclusivity_score": 0.97,
            "oligo_quality_score": 0.92,
            "outbreak_sequence_score": 0.90,
            "overall_rank": 3,
            "decision": "do not advance without redesign",
        },
    ]
)

candidate_scores
Suggested ranking components:

Target conservation: fraction of target sequences without critical mismatches.
Near-neighbor separation: absence of plausible non-target amplicons.
Oligo physical quality: absence of strong self-complementarity, primer dimers, extreme GC, or problematic runs.
Outbreak robustness: no critical mismatch in current outbreak sequences.
Manufacturability: avoids unusual modifications unless justified.
Change-control burden: target region is stable enough for surveillance.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
14 Analytical Validation Plan
After in-silico review, a real molecular diagnostic assay would need laboratory validation. This notebook does not provide protocols, but the validation plan would normally address:







Performance characteristic
Question




Limit of detection
What is the lowest concentration detected with acceptable probability in each claimed specimen type?


Inclusivity
Does the assay detect representative target diversity?


Exclusivity / cross-reactivity
Does the assay avoid detection of near neighbors and likely co-occurring organisms?


Precision
Are results reproducible across operators, instruments, reagent lots, days, and sites?


Interference
Do substances or specimen matrix effects inhibit detection?


Stability
Are specimens, controls, and reagents stable under claimed conditions?


Controls
Do extraction, amplification, inhibition, positive, and negative controls behave as expected?


Clinical evaluation
Does assay performance support the intended use in the target population?



CLSI MM03 describes molecular diagnostic methods for infectious diseases, including sequence selection, performance evaluation, inhibitors, false-positive control, interpretation, quality assurance, and regulatory considerations. CLSI MM17 addresses validation and verification concepts for qualitative multiplex nucleic acid assays. Those standards are useful anchors for turning an in-silico design into a regulated diagnostic-development plan.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
15 Regulatory and Quality Considerations
An industry or clinical laboratory team should separate four claims:

In-silico design claim: the target window is predicted to be inclusive and exclusive based on the current sequence database.
Analytical performance claim: the assay detects target material under controlled laboratory conditions.
Clinical performance claim: the assay identifies infected patients in the intended population and specimen type.
Public health utility claim: the assay supports outbreak response, triage, surveillance, or contact investigation.

Only the first claim can be supported by this notebook. The other claims require laboratory and clinical evidence.
Version control should capture:

source databases and access dates,
accession lists and exclusion criteria,
alignment and tree software versions,
primer/probe design software and parameters,
candidate scoring rules,
review sign-off,
periodic re-analysis triggers when new sequences appear.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
16 Failure Modes and Mitigations








Failure mode
How it could happen
Mitigation




False negative from sequence drift
Mutation in primer/probe binding site
Multi-sequence inclusivity screen and periodic surveillance


False negative from incomplete sampling
Missing lineage absent from databases
Conservative target choice and documented uncertainty


False positive from near neighbor
Related hantavirus amplified
Near-neighbor exclusivity panel and in-silico PCR


False positive from contamination
Amplicon or control contamination
Workflow controls and physical separation in real labs


Inhibition
Specimen matrix suppresses amplification
Internal control and specimen validation


Overclaiming
In-silico pass treated as clinical validation
Clear claim boundaries and regulatory review

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
18 Discussion
The MV Hondius cluster is a strong case study because it shows how modern molecular diagnostics sits between public health and molecular biology. Phylogeny helps define the target and near-neighbor space. Epidemiology defines who might be tested, what specimens might be relevant, and what turnaround time would matter. Assay design translates sequence conservation into a practical screen, while inclusivity and exclusivity testing force the developer to state what the assay is, and is not, designed to detect.
The most important scientific discipline in this exercise is restraint. A sequence-perfect candidate is not a clinical test. A beautiful phylogenetic tree does not prove transmission. A negative in-silico cross-reactivity search does not replace wet-lab validation. For molecular diagnostics, evidence must be layered: genomic rationale first, analytical performance second, clinical performance third, and public-health utility last.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
text:
19 Reproducibility Checklist

Define intended target scope: Andes virus only, outbreak lineage, or broad hantavirus screen.
Freeze accession lists and database versions.
Curate sequences by segment, quality, geography, host, and date.
Align S, M, and L segments separately.
Build segment-specific phylogenies.
Identify conserved target windows.
Rank primer/probe candidates without publishing operational sequences.
Run in-silico inclusivity against target diversity.
Run in-silico exclusivity against near neighbors and background organisms.
Document limitations and candidate-selection rationale.
Define the laboratory validation plan without claiming validation.
Add periodic sequence-surveillance triggers.

title: Hantavirus Phylogeny, Epidemiology, and Hypothetical TaqMan Assay Design
